document.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance Program Guide

root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance Program Guide
root.summary: Front matter and overview for the Cúram 8.1–8.1.2 Income Support for Medical Assistance guide. Explains the rule-based eligibility framework used to determine medical assistance coverage, including cascading eligibility, non-financial requirements, household composition, income/resource rules, deeming, deductions, and coverage-specific financial limits and periods. Covers major program types such as LIFC, transitional and extended LIFC, children, pregnant women, newborn, Title IV-E foster/adoption, medically needy, refugee, ABD, Medicare cost-sharing programs (QMB/SLMB/QI/QDWI), breast/cervical cancer, emergency medical assistance, and retroactive requests. Also includes edition information, table of contents, and legal front matter: notices, privacy policy, trademarks, and copyright.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This note tells readers to review the Notices section before using the product or this information. It serves as a brief legal and cautionary preface for the document, with copyright attribution to Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Front matter for the Medical Assistance guide. States this edition applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, with Merative copyright/trademark notices. The contents page lists major topics: program overview, rule sets, non-financial and financial eligibility rules, coverage types (LIFC, ABD, children, pregnant women, newborn, refugee, QMB/SLMB/QDWI, BCC, emergency, medically needy, long-term care), notices, privacy, and trademarks. The overview explains Medical Assistance eligibility, evidence-based rules, and the cascading eligibility hierarchy, including post-Jan. 1, 2014 changes and removed programs.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Overview of Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance (Traditional) common eligibility rules. Covers emergency coverage types, citizenship/non-citizen and 5-year alien eligibility exceptions, SSN and residency requirements, dependent child rules (caretaker relative, age limits, deprivation and unemployment/underemployment), alien sponsor deeming exceptions, and family resource/income calculation methods including deeming between spouses, parents, and sponsors. Useful for rules-based eligibility and financial determination details.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: Covers adult/Traditional Medical Assistance income and resource rules, including deeming for spouse-to-spouse, parent-to-child, and sponsor-to-alien cases. Details income deductions/disregards ($90 work allowance, LIFC $30 and 1/3 or $30, dependent care, child support/alimony) and SSI/deeming exceptions. Explains adult resource calculations for eligible individuals, couples, children, and ineligible spouses, plus resource deeming limits and exclusions. Also outlines full income calculation workflows for adult coverage types, including sponsor deemed income and in-kind support maintenance.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: This section covers Medical Assistance eligibility rules for income, household composition, and special LIFC cases. It explains income deductions (general $20 disregard, self-employment loss, $65 deduction, impairment-related expenses, 1/2 remaining earned income), LIFC household membership and exclusions, temporary absence, resource and income limits, and transitional LIFC coverage (up to 12 months with reporting requirements, good cause, reinstatement). It also begins extended LIFC coverage rules for four months after closure due to child support income.

children.title: Edition — pages 45-54
children.summary: Covers Cúram Medical Assistance rules for several coverage types: extended LIFC due to child/spousal support changes and residency limits; Children’s Medical Assistance (under 6 and 6–19) with household, financial unit, resource caps, and income thresholds; Pregnant Woman MA including pregnancy, financial unit, resource/income rules, and 60-day postpartum coverage; Newborn MA deemed eligibility for 1 year; Title IV-E adoption/foster care MA; Medically Needy children and pregnant women; and Refugee MA eligibility, household composition, alien/qualified status, 8-month limit, resources, and income.

children.title: Edition — pages 55-64
children.summary: Covers Medical Assistance eligibility rules for Refugee Medical Assistance and Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD) categories. Includes non-financial, alien status, coverage periods, financial unit, household size, resource limits, and income exclusions for Refugee Medical Assistance. Then details ABD eligibility groups: SSI recipients, 1619(b), Pickle People, Disabled Adult Children, Disabled Widow(er)s, and children who received SSI on Aug. 22, 1996, with their financial units, household-size rules, resource limits, and income-test basics.

children.title: Edition — pages 65-74
children.summary: Pages 65–74 cover Medical Assistance coverage rules: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiaries (SLMB), Qualified Individual (QI-1), Qualified Disabled Working Individuals (QDWI), Breast and Cervical Cancer assistance, and Emergency Medical Assistance. Each program details eligibility requirements, financial-unit composition, household-size exclusions, resource limits, and income thresholds. Key limits include QMB/SLMB/QI income bands tied to Medicare Part A and poverty levels, QDWI for disabled workers under 65, and Breast/Cervical Cancer rules for uninsured women under 65. Emergency Medical Assistance is introduced at the end.

children.title: Edition — pages 75-78
children.summary: This section covers Emergency Medical Assistance eligibility and coverage rules, plus retroactive Medical Assistance requests. It lists emergency coverage types and qualifying conditions, including citizenship, residency, emergency medical need, and coverage period rules. It explains the 3-month retroactive period before application, eligibility requirements by month, evidence requirements, and an option controlling the retroactive start date. It also describes how retroactive checks can be triggered from application or integrated case and notes exclusions for CHIP and QMB.

Front matter and overview for the Medical Assistance guide in Cúram 8.1–8.1.2. Describes the rule-based eligibility framework, cascading eligibility, and major coverage types: traditional/LIFC, children, pregnant women, newborn, adoption/foster care, medically needy, refugee, ABD, Medicare-related programs (QMB/SLMB/QI/QDWI), breast/cervical cancer, emergency assistance, and retroactive requests. Includes common non-financial rules, resource/income deeming, household composition, income deductions, and coverage-specific financial limits and periods. Also contains edition, notices, privacy, and trademarks.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This page contains legal notices: fictitious-name disclaimer for examples, copyright/license terms for sample source programs, warranty/liability disclaimers, a link to Merative’s privacy policy, and trademark attributions for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle, Linux, Microsoft, UNIX, and others.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: This section lists legal notices for the document, including sample data disclaimers, copyright license terms for sample programs, the Merative privacy policy link, and trademark attributions. It identifies trademarks for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX, and notes that other names may belong to third parties.

Merative’s notices and permission terms for using the publication: personal use is allowed if proprietary notices are preserved; enterprise use is allowed internally only, with no derivatives or external distribution without consent. It disclaims warranties, liability, and guarantees; reserves rights to withdraw permission; and requires compliance with export laws. It also notes possible patent coverage, states non-Merative product information is from external sources and unverified, and says external website references are for convenience only. Copyright: © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance (Traditional) Program manual. Lists front matter and Chapter 1 topics with page numbers, including overview, common rules, family/adult income and resource rules, LIFC and transitional/extended coverage, children, pregnant women, newborn, Title IV-E foster/adoption, medically needy, refugee, ABD, Medicare-related assistance programs, breast/cervical cancer, emergency medical assistance, and retroactive requests. Includes notices, privacy policy, and trademarks.

children.title: 1 Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance (Traditional) Program overview
1.1 Overview of Medical Assistance Rule Sets
Medical Assistance Coverage Types
children.summary: Overview of Medical Assistance coverage types supported by Cúram Income Support and the rule sets used to determine eligibility. Lists the main coverage groups: mandatory categorically needy (LIFC, ABD, pregnant women, newborns, children, Title IV-E foster care/adoption), cost sharing (QMB, SLMB, Qualified Individual, QDWI), emergency Medical Assistance, optional categorically needy (refugee, BCC), medically needy, spend down, and long-term care. Notes that some groups are not evaluated for new applications since Jan. 1, 2014 due to MAGI-based Medicaid.

children.title: Medical Assistance Eligibility Rules
children.summary: This section explains the Medical Assistance eligibility framework and how rules are organized. It distinguishes coverage type eligibility from financial eligibility, with financial rules further split into income and resource rules. It notes that non-financial requirements (citizenship, residency, SSN) are covered elsewhere, and that coverage type rules define assistance units, financial units, household size, and dependent child status. It also says financial rules determine countable/excluded resources and income, including deductions, by coverage type.

children.title: Cascading Eligibility
children.summary: Explains cascading eligibility for Medical Assistance, where coverage types are evaluated by priority and lower levels are skipped once a client qualifies at a higher level. Describes the ACA-driven change to the Income Support Medical Assistance cascade effective Jan. 1, 2014, with different hierarchies for pre-2014 and post-2014 applications and ongoing cases after a change of circumstance. Notes coverage types removed from the cascade in 2014 and clarifies that CHIP eligibility is determined separately and is not part of the cascade.

Overview of Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance rule sets. Explains which coverage types are supported, including mandatory categorically needy, cost sharing, emergency, optional categorically needy, medically needy/spend down, and long-term care, with note that some groups are not evaluated for new applications since Jan. 1, 2014 due to MAGI Medicaid. Describes the rule structure: coverage type eligibility vs financial eligibility, plus common non-financial requirements. Also outlines cascading eligibility, including post-2014 ACA changes, removed programs, and separate CHIP handling.

children.title: 1.2 Common Rules - Non-Financial Requirements
Citizenship
children.summary: Defines Medicaid citizenship rules under non-financial eligibility. An individual must be a U.S. citizen, U.S. non-citizen national, or eligible alien. It explains who counts as a qualified alien, including refugees, asylees, LPRs, parolees, Cuban/Haitian entrants, Amerasians, certain trafficking victims, Canadian-born American Indians, some Hmong/Laotian-related relatives, and battered aliens. It also covers qualified alien eligibility timing: pre-8/22/1996 cases, continuous residence exceptions, and the 5-year bar for post-8/22/1996 entrants plus its exceptions.

children.title: Social Security Number
children.summary: Explains Social Security Number requirements for eligibility. An individual must either provide an SSN or apply for one. Lists exceptions: newborn children under Medical Assistance, adopted children without an SSN until adoption is finalized, and household members not included in the assistance unit. This section is useful for determining when an SSN is required and who is exempt.

children.title: Residency
children.summary: Covers Medicaid residency rules and related eligibility basics. Includes qualified alien categories, SSN requirements and exceptions, and detailed state residency rules for individuals under 21 and age 21+, including non-institutionalized cases, intent, incapacity to state intent, special situations (state supplementary payments and Title IV-E foster/adoption assistance), and change-in-residency limits. Also notes a person must be alive and cannot receive Medical Assistance in more than one state.

Covers non-financial Medical Assistance requirements: Citizenship, Residency, and SSN. Citizenship rules define eligible U.S. citizens, U.S. non-citizen nationals, and qualified/eligible aliens, including listed alien categories, battered aliens, and 5-year bar exceptions. SSN rules require an SSN or application, with exceptions for newborns, adopted children before finalization, and non-included household members. Residency rules cover under-21 and 21+ non-institutionalized cases, intent/incapacity, special state-payment and Title IV-E situations, plus limits on multiple-state coverage and deceased members.

children.title: 1.3 Common Rules - Dependent Child
Deprivation of Support
children.summary: Defines when a child is “deprived of support” for Medical Assistance eligibility. Covers dependency requirements: living with a non-parent caretaker relative, age limits (under 18 or under 19 in qualifying full-time education), and deprivation of parental support. Lists deprivation reasons: death, continued absence, parental incapacity, and unemployment/underemployment of the principal wage earner. Also explains what counts as continued absence (e.g., divorce, incarceration, deportation) and what does not, plus rules for unemployment/underemployment and good-cause refusals of work.

Defines dependent child rules for Medical Assistance: child must live with a non-parent caretaker relative, meet age limits (under 18, or under 19 if single and in full-time secondary/GED/vocational training), and be deprived of parental support. Explains qualifying caretaker relatives and deprivation reasons: death, continued absence, parental incapacity, or unemployment/underemployment of the principal wage earner. Details what counts as continued absence, principal wage earner status, unemployment timing, and good-cause refusals of work or training.

children.title: 1.4 Common Rules - Alien Sponsor
children.summary: Defines alien sponsor rules for deeming in Cúram. A sponsor is someone who signed an Affidavit of Support for an alien’s U.S. entry, is not in the assistance unit, and whose income/resources are deemed to the alien; an alien may have multiple sponsors. Lists deeming exceptions where sponsor income/resources are not counted, including refugees, refugee parolees, parolees, asylees, asylee parolees, special immigrants, Cuban entrants, Haitian entrants, and LPRs who adjusted from refugee or asylee status.

children.title: 1.5 Family - Common Resource Rules
children.summary: Explains how to calculate family coverage group resources. It defines total countable resources as an individual’s countable resources plus any deemed resources, then combined across household members. It points to coverage-type-specific resource limits elsewhere. It also outlines resource deeming rules for three cases: spouse-to-spouse deeming for an ineligible spouse of an eligible non-parent caretaker relative, parent-to-child deeming for a parent not in the LIFC assistance unit, and sponsor-to-alien deeming.

children.title: 1.6 Family - Common Income Rules
children.summary: Covers family coverage income rules: how to calculate total countable income across a household, including gross unearned/earned income, self-employment, deemable income, gross income tests (LIFC), and net income after deductions. Explains deeming rules for spouse-to-spouse, parent-to-child, and sponsor-to-alien, plus exceptions (e.g., SSI, refugee payments, tribal payments, certain support, VA benefits, no residence). Also lists income deductions/disregards: $90 work allowance, LIFC $30 and 1/3 or $30 disregard, dependent care allowance, and child support/alimony deductions.

children.title: 1.7 Adult - Common Resource Rules
Total Countable Resources
children.summary: Explains how to determine total countable resources for adult coverage types in ABD and other adult programs. It defines the financial unit as one of four cases: eligible individual with no spouse, eligible child, eligible individual with ineligible spouse, or eligible couple. For each case, it outlines the required resource calculation and any deeming steps, including sponsor-to-alien, parent-to-child, and spouse-to-spouse deeming, which are added to the household’s countable resources.

children.title: Resource Deeming
children.summary: Explains how resource deeming works for eligibility determinations. Covers which individuals’ resources are deemed based on household rules: spouse-to-spouse, parent-to-child, and sponsor-to-alien. Notes key exceptions: SSI recipients’ resources are never deemed, deeming does not apply when a family has no residence, and certain pension funds/IRAs/Keogh accounts owned by an ineligible spouse or parent are excluded. Also outlines steps for combining eligible spouse resources and calculating deemed parent resources for blind/disabled children.

Explains adult coverage resource calculation rules for ABD and other adult programs. Covers total countable resources for four financial unit cases: eligible individual with no spouse, eligible child, eligible individual with ineligible spouse, and eligible couple. Includes sponsor-to-alien, spouse-to-spouse, and parent-to-child deeming, plus how eligible couple resources are combined. Notes key exceptions: SSI recipients’ resources are never deemed, deeming does not apply without a residence, and certain pension funds/IRAs/Keogh accounts owned by an ineligible spouse or parent are excluded.

children.title: 1.8 Adult - Common Income Rules
Total Countable Income
children.summary: Procedures for calculating total countable income for ABD and other adult coverage types. Covers the financial unit groups: eligible individual with no spouse, eligible child, eligible couple, and eligible individual with an ineligible spouse. Explains step-by-step treatment of in-kind support and maintenance, unearned and earned income, deductions, self-employment income, sponsored aliens, and deeming from sponsors or parents. Includes eligibility checks against SSI Federal Payment Standard/FBR and when to apply sponsor-to-alien or parent-to-child deeming.

children.title: Income Deeming
children.summary: Rules for income deeming across spouse-to-spouse, parent-to-child, and sponsor-to-alien cases. Explains who is subject to deeming, the order of calculations, and how deemed earned/unearned income is added to the eligible person’s income. Covers exceptions: SSI recipient income is never deemed; several income types are excluded; ISM rules apply. Includes spouse living allowance, parent allocations, sponsor allocations, and ineligible child deductions. Also outlines sponsor deemed income steps and how to total net countable income.

children.title: Income Deductions
children.summary: Explains how income deductions are applied for individuals in the financial unit whose income is counted. Covers ordering of disregards for employed/self-employed people and notes that deductions stop when no income remains. Describes unearned income deductions, including combining spouses’/parents’ income before applying deductions, and the $20 General Income Disregard with exceptions for Tribal General Assistance, Refugee Act payments, VA Pension, and VA Compensation to a parent. Begins earned income deductions, including the remaining $20 disregard and self-employment loss.

Adult common income rules for ABD and other adult coverage types. Covers total countable income by financial unit (individual, child, couple, ineligible spouse), including ISM, unearned/earned income, self-employment, and sponsor or parent deeming. Explains deeming rules and exceptions (SSI income not deemed; certain payments excluded). Also details spouse-to-spouse, parent-to-child, and sponsor-to-alien deeming, plus ineligible child allocations. Ends with income deduction order, including the $20 disregard and self-employment loss treatment.

children.title: 1.9 Low Income Families with Children Medical Assistance Rules
Household Composition
Temporary Absence
children.summary: This section explains when a household member who is away from home may still count for Medical Assistance. It says absence can be temporary, and eligibility may continue if all other program rules are met and there is a definite plan for the person to return home. It follows earlier household-composition rules for minor parents, optional household members, and pregnant women.

Explains how Medical Assistance household composition is determined for Low Income Families with Children (LIFC). It defines mandatory and optional household members, including dependent child, parent, and sibling rules, plus exceptions for a disabled parent and minor parent living with parents/non-parent caretaker relatives. It also covers pregnant women in the last trimester with an unborn child, and temporary absence, which may still allow a member to count if there is a definite return plan and other eligibility rules are met.

children.title: Household Determination
children.summary: Defines household determination rules for Medical Assistance. Covers temporary absence rules: absent members may remain eligible up to 180 days, with extensions for institutional care or education/training away from home. Identifies non-household members excluded from the assistance unit, including SSI recipients and children receiving foster care or adoption assistance; their income/resources are not counted. Also lists excluded household members who are in the home, are ineligible, but are counted in household size and income/resources, such as sanctioned parents, certain ABD recipients, TPL noncompliance, citizenship/residency/SSN failures, and IPV/work-sanctioned parents.

children.title: Resource Rules
children.summary: Explains household resource and income eligibility rules. Resources are classified as countable or non-countable, liquid or non-liquid; only countable resources count toward limits of $2,000 for a 1-person household, $3,000 for 2 members, or $3,000 plus $25 for each additional member. Also summarizes countable income rules: gross countable income must be at or below 185% of the monthly limit and net countable income at or below 100% for household size. Includes exceptions for excluded household members and deemed-member income/resources.

children.title: Income Rules
children.summary: Explains how household income is counted for eligibility. Defines countable vs non-countable unearned, earned, and self-employment income, and states two limits that must both be met: gross countable income must be at or below 185% of the monthly limit for household size, and net countable income must be at or below 100% of that monthly limit. Useful for checking income eligibility rules and thresholds.

Defines LIFC Medical Assistance eligibility rules. Covers assistance-unit formation and household composition: dependent child, parent, sibling, optional caretaker-relative members, minor parent/parent exceptions, unborn child in last trimester, pregnant woman/spouse, and temporary absence. Explains household determination: non-household members (SSI, foster/adoption assistance) excluded; certain ineligible members counted in size/income; deemed income/resources for aliens, minor parent parents, and NPCR spouses. Also gives resource limits ($2,000/$3,000 + $25) and income limits (gross ≤185%, net ≤100%).

children.title: 1.10 Transitional LIFC Medical Assistance Rules
Transitional Medical Assistance Eligibility Determination
Household Composition
children.summary: This section explains Transitional Medical Assistance household composition rules. It covers which members remain eligible after Low Income Families with Children Medical Assistance ends, how new household members may be added if they would have qualified under current application rules, and that members who leave lose eligibility. It also outlines the 12-month Transitional Medical Assistance period, split into two six-month periods, including first-period rules (dependent child required, no income/resource test, residency loss/resumption) and the requirement to receive the first period to qualify for the second.

Explains Transitional LIFC Medical Assistance eligibility. A household may receive up to 12 months of extended coverage after losing LIFC if it was eligible and received LIFC in 3 of the prior 6 months, and ineligibility results from increased earnings of a caretaker relative or loss of the $30/$30-and-1/3 disregard. It also covers mixed-change cases where earnings changes plus other factors may still qualify, especially when the earnings increase alone would cause ineligibility.

children.title: Transitional Medical Assistance Eligibility Period
children.summary: Explains Transitional Medical Assistance eligibility when earnings cause loss of Low Income Families with Children Medical Assistance. Covers who remains eligible in the household, new household members, and removal of members. Describes the 12-month coverage period split into two six-month periods, starting the month after LFCA ends. First six months have no income or resource test, but the household must include at least one dependent child; moving out of state can end eligibility, though residency can be resumed within that period. Second six months require prior receipt throughout the first period.

children.title: Income Reporting Requirements
children.summary: Explains Transitional Medical Assistance income reporting rules. Households must report gross earnings quarterly. For the first 3 months, the caretaker relative must have earnings or good cause for no earnings; if no earnings are reported, this must be reported by the 21st of month 4. For months 4–6 and 7–9, the household must report caretaker earnings, total monthly earnings, and dependent care costs by the 21st of months 7 and 10. Failure to meet reporting rules can end eligibility.

children.title: Reinstatement of Transitional Medical Assistance
children.summary: Explains when Transitional Medical Assistance can be reinstated after a household regains and later loses LIFC during the transitional period. If LIFC resumes, transitional coverage stops but the original time continues to run. If LIFC is lost again, the household may qualify either for a new transitional benefit period or for the remaining time on the original period, if eligibility conditions are met. Also shows related reporting rules and good-cause exceptions for failure to report.

Defines Transitional LIFC Medical Assistance, a 12-month extension after LIFC ends due to a caretaker relative’s increased earnings or loss of the $30/$30-and-1/3 disregard. Covers eligibility rules (3 of prior 6 months on LIFC), mixed-change cases, household composition changes, and the two six-month eligibility periods. The first six months have no income/resource test but require a dependent child and residency; the second requires continued receipt, dependent child, income thresholds, and quarterly earnings reporting. Includes good-cause exceptions and reinstatement rules.

children.title: 1.11 Extended LIFC Medical Assistance Rules
children.summary: Explains Extended LIFC Medical Assistance: when absent-parent child support payments make a child ineligible, coverage continues automatically for 4 months. Coverage starts the first day of the month after closure and runs for four consecutive months. Eligibility requires the household to have been eligible for and receiving LIFC Medical Assistance in at least 3 of the 6 months before losing eligibility. Useful for determining whether a case qualifies for post-closure extended medical coverage.

children.title: 1.12 Children Medical Assistance Rules
Common Rules
children.summary: Common Rules defines the shared eligibility rules for children under 6 and children aged 6–19, excluding the income test. It lists household requirements: the child must meet non-financial requirements, not be an SSI recipient, and not receive foster care or adoption assistance payments; the household must also pass the resource test. It explains the financial unit, including the child, parents in the home, qualifying dependent siblings, and sponsored alien sponsor/spouse if applicable, plus exceptions and household size rules.

children.title: Income Rules
children.summary: This section states the income rules for Medical Assistance (Traditional) children’s coverage. Only countable income is used to determine eligibility. Children under 6 must have net countable income at or below 133% of the federal poverty level for household size; children ages 6–9 must be at or below 100% FPL. It also notes that income is classified as countable/non-countable and points to common income rules for calculation steps.

Children’s Medical Assistance covers children up to age 19, with two groups: under 6 and ages 6–19; rules are the same except for income. Common Rules require the child to meet non-financial rules, not be an SSI recipient, and not receive foster care or adoption assistance, and the household must pass the resource test. The financial unit includes the child, parents in the home, qualifying dependent siblings, and any sponsor/spouse for sponsored aliens. Household size excludes the sponsor/spouse. Income rules are handled separately.

children.title: 1.13 Pregnant Women Medical Assistance Rules
children.summary: Pregnant Woman Medical Assistance covers pregnant women within income/resource limits. Eligibility requires pregnancy, non-financial requirements, not being an SSI recipient, not a foster care or adoption assistance child, and passing both resource and income tests. The financial unit includes the woman, unborn child(ren), spouse, dependent blood/adoptive siblings, sponsor/spouse for sponsored aliens, and major parents for certain minors. Excludes SSI recipients and foster/adoption assistance children. Household size excludes major parents and alien sponsor/spouse. Income changes do not affect eligibility once determined.

children.title: 1.14 Newborn Medical Assistance Rules
children.summary: Newborns born to a mother who is eligible for and receiving Medical Assistance on the date of birth are automatically deemed eligible for Medical Assistance for up to 1 year. They are treated as having applied and been found eligible on the birth date and do not have to meet income or resource rules. This section sits between general Medical Assistance eligibility rules (resources, income, postpartum coverage) and the next section on Title IV-E Adoption Medical Assistance.

children.title: 1.15 Title IV-E Adoption Medical Assistance Rules
children.summary: Explains that children receiving Title IV-E Adoption Assistance Payments are categorically needy and eligible for Medical Assistance. Briefly defines Title IV-E as part of the Social Security Act covering federal foster care and adoption assistance, and points to the Non-Financial Requirements section for additional eligibility details.

children.title: 1.16 Title IV-E Foster Care Medical Assistance Rules
children.summary: Children receiving Title IV-E foster care maintenance payments are categorically needy and eligible for Medical Assistance. The section also notes that children receiving Title IV-E adoption assistance payments are eligible. It points to non-financial requirements for additional eligibility details. This node is useful for determining Medicaid eligibility tied to federal foster care or adoption assistance under Title IV-E.

children.title: 1.17 Medically Needy Children Medical Assistance
children.summary: This section defines eligibility for Children’s Medically Needy Medical Assistance. It covers children under 19 who do not qualify for regular Medical Assistance due to income/resources. Key rules: must meet non-financial and dependent child age/caretaker rules, not be an SSI recipient, and not receive foster care or adoption assistance payments. It outlines the financial unit, household size exceptions for alien sponsors, deemed sponsor income, and resource/income limits, including countable resources thresholds and the Medically Needy Income Level at 133% FPL.

children.title: 1.18 Medically Needy Pregnant Women Medical Assistance
children.summary: Rules for Medically Needy Medicaid eligibility for pregnant women. Covers who qualifies during pregnancy if otherwise Medicaid-eligible except income/resources, required non-financial and financial tests, and categorical-needy pregnancy criteria (pregnant, not SSI, not in foster/adoption assistance, and does not leave the state). Also defines the financial unit/household members whose income/resources count, including unborn child, father if in home, dependent siblings, sponsor/spouse for sponsored aliens, and minor pregnant woman’s parents, with exclusions for SSI and foster/adoption assistance recipients.

children.title: 1.19 Refugee Medical Assistance Rules
Household Composition
children.summary: Defines which household members are included in Refugee Medical Assistance and whose income/resources count. The assistance unit includes the eligible refugee individual, an eligible spouse, dependent children meeting US-born child RMA rules, and certain minor children of those dependents. It also restates adult and child RMA qualifying conditions, including no other MA/CHIP coverage, meeting non-financial conditions, age limits for adults, and child dependency requirements.

children.title: Refugee Medical Assistance Coverage Period
children.summary: Explains Refugee Medical Assistance coverage period rules. The time limit applies to each individual, not the case. Eligibility starts either on the first day of the month the person enters the U.S. with qualifying alien status, or on the first day of the month the person later receives qualifying status. The eligibility period lasts 8 full months from that start date, ending on the last day of the eighth month. Useful for determining when RMA coverage begins and ends.

children.title: Alien Status
children.summary: Defines Alien Status eligibility for Refugee Medical Assistance. An individual must have qualified alien status and be eligible from the first day of the application month through the end of the RMA eligibility period. Covers cases where the person qualified on entry to the US or after entry. Lists eligible alien statuses on entry and current statuses, including refugee, asylee, parolee, withholding of deportation, Cuban/Haitian entrant, Amerasian immigrant, trafficking victim, certain Afghan/Iraqi special immigrants and dependents, and lawful permanent residents with prior qualifying status.

children.title: Financial Unit
children.summary: Defines the refugee Medical Assistance financial unit and household size. Include the refugee, spouse in the home, dependent children meeting child rules (except deprivation), and a minor parent’s child if applicable. Exclude SSI recipients and children receiving foster care or adoption assistance from the assistance unit and resource count. Explains countable resource limits: under $2,000 for 1 person, under $3,000 for 2 people, or $3,000 plus $25 per additional member. Also lists excluded refugee resettlement cash grants and voluntary agency R&P cash assistance.

children.title: Resource Rules
children.summary: Defines Refugee Medical Assistance resource eligibility. Countable household resources must be under $2,000 for 1 member, $3,000 for 2 members, or $3,000 plus $25 for each additional member. The resource rules apply to the household/financial unit and point to common family resource calculations. It also excludes from resource calculations refugee resettlement cash grants from State/Justice reception and placement programs and R&P cash assistance from voluntary resettlement agencies.

children.title: Income Rules
children.summary: States the income rules for Medically Needy Children Medical Assistance: household net countable income must be at or below 200% of the Federal Poverty Level, with a reference to the common income calculation rules. Also notes income exclusions for Refugee Medical Assistance, including Match Grant payments and Refugee Cash Assistance payments, which are not counted as income. The excerpt then begins the next section on Aged, Blind, and Disabled Medical Assistance eligibility.

Rules for Refugee Medical Assistance eligibility. Covers required household composition, adult/child qualifying conditions, non-financial conditions, alien status, 8-month individual coverage period, financial unit/household size, and resource rules. The assistance unit includes the refugee, spouse in home, qualifying dependent children, and some minor children; SSI, foster care, and adoption assistance recipients are excluded. Countable resources must stay under $2,000/$3,000 plus $25 per extra member, and certain refugee resettlement/R&P cash payments are excluded.

children.title: 1.20 Aged, Blind, and Disabled Medical Assistance Rules
ABD Categories
Section 1619(b) eligible
children.summary: Explains eligibility for Medical Assistance under Section 1619(b): individuals must have SSA-determined 1619(b) status and are categorically financially eligible, with no income or resource tests if other eligibility factors are met. This section also begins the Disabled Adult Children (DAC) category, outlining that adults 18+ disabled before age 22 may qualify if they receive Title II child’s benefits on a parent’s record, lost SSI after July 1, 1987 due to those benefits, and meet DAC resource and income tests.

children.title: Disabled Adult Children
children.summary: Explains Disabled Adult Children (DAC) as a protected Medical Assistance category tied to Title II child’s insurance benefits on a parent’s record. A person age 18+ may qualify if disability/blindness began before age 22, they receive or are entitled to DAC benefits, and they lost SSI after July 1, 1987 due to those benefits or increases. DACs must meet resource and income tests, though income testing is unnecessary if DAC benefits are the only income. Also notes the DAC financial unit begins with the disabled adult child alone if no spouse.

children.title: Disabled Widow(er)s not receiving Medicare
children.summary: Explains Medicaid eligibility for Disabled Widow(er)s not receiving Medicare Part A: must receive Social Security disabled widow(er) benefits, be age 60–64, have lost SSI due to widow(er) Title II benefits, and be blind or disabled, plus meet resource and income tests. Defines the financial unit for no spouse, eligible couple, and ineligible spouse cases, including alien sponsor deeming rules and exclusion of SSI recipients. Also gives resource and income household size exceptions.

children.title: Children Who Had Received SSI on August 22, 1996
children.summary: Explains Medicaid eligibility for children who were receiving SSI on Aug. 22, 1996 and lost SSI after July 1, 1997 due to the stricter childhood disability rules. Lists required conditions: under 18, prior SSI on that date, termination under P.L. 104-193 criteria, still meeting pre-1996 disability rules, and satisfying resource and income tests. Defines the financial unit (disabled child, living parent(s), and certain alien sponsor income; exclude SSI recipients). Also introduces Pickle Amendment coverage for certain former SSI recipients receiving Social Security benefits.

children.title: Pickle People (Pickle Amendment Coverage)
children.summary: Explains two Medicaid eligibility pathways: children who received SSI on Aug. 22, 1996 and lost SSI under the 1996 disability rules but kept Medicaid if age, SSI timing, resource, and income criteria are met; and “Pickle People” (Pickle Amendment coverage), people who lost SSI after April 1977 due to COLA-related income increases while still receiving Social Security. Also defines Pickle financial units and resource household size rules, including treatment of parents, spouses, aliens, and SSI recipients.

Defines Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD) Medical Assistance eligibility. Covers general requirements (non-financial rules, ABD category, resource and income tests), SSI recipients, and protected/deemed SSI groups: 1619(b), Disabled Adult Children, Disabled Widow(er)s not receiving Medicare, Children who received SSI on Aug. 22, 1996, and Pickle People. Each category has specific age/disability/benefit-loss criteria plus financial unit, household size, and sponsor/spouse deeming rules; 1619(b) has no income or resource test.

children.title: Resource Rules
children.summary: Defines ABD resource and income eligibility rules. Countable resources are limited to $2,000 for an individual or $3,000 for a couple for Pickle People, DAC, and disabled widows(er)s not receiving Medicare; children who received SSI on Aug. 22, 1996 are limited to $2,000 individual. No resource test applies to 1619(b). Explains that only countable resources/income are used, and refers to common resource rules for calculations. Starts income rules, including Pickle People meeting the test if income is below the current SSI income limit.

children.title: Income Rules
children.summary: This section explains ABD income rules and how household income is counted. It states that only countable unearned, earned, and self-employment income is used for eligibility, and notes there is no income test for 1619(b). For Pickle People, an individual passes the income test if income is below the current SSI income limit. It also references household size exceptions relevant to income determination, such as excluding SSI recipients, sponsors, parents in the home, or an ineligible spouse when no deemable income exists.

Defines Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD) Medical Assistance eligibility rules. Covers non-financial requirements plus ABD categories: SSI recipients, 1619(b), DAC, disabled widow(er)s not on Medicare, children who received SSI on Aug. 22, 1996, and Pickle People. Explains category-specific eligibility, financial units and household-size rules, sponsor/spouse deeming, and resource/income tests. Key limits: $2,000 individual/$3,000 couple for Pickle, DAC, and disabled widow(er)s; $2,000 individual for children; no resource or income test for 1619(b).

children.title: 1.21 Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries Medical Assistance
Financial Unit
children.summary: This section defines the financial unit for Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMB) medical assistance. It explains that the financial unit may be: an eligible individual with no spouse, an eligible child, an eligible couple, or an eligible individual with an ineligible spouse. For a single Medicare Part A recipient, the unit includes the individual and, if applicable, sponsor income for aliens (except SSI recipients). For an eligible child, it includes the child and parent(s) when the child lives with them.

children.title: Resource Rules
children.summary: Explains resource eligibility rules for the household: only countable resources (liquid or non-liquid) are considered, and they must stay below set limits. The limits are $6,940 for an individual and $10,410 for a couple. Also notes the general classification of resources as countable or non-countable for determining eligibility.

children.title: Income Rules
children.summary: Defines Income Rules for the Medical Assistance (Traditional) program: only countable income is used, and household income must be at or below 100% of the federal poverty level. Introduces rules for Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiaries (SLMB), which covers people with Medicare Part A whose income is above QMB but at or below 120% of poverty. Lists required eligibility conditions and begins financial unit rules, including eligible individual with no spouse and sponsor income treatment for certain aliens.

Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMB) Medical Assistance covers people with Medicare Part A whose income is below 100% of poverty. Eligibility requires non-financial requirements, Medicare Part A, a resource test, and an income test. The section defines financial units for a single individual, child, couple, or person with an ineligible spouse, including sponsor income rules for certain aliens. It also states household size exceptions and resource limits: $6,940 for an individual and $10,410 for a couple, counting only countable resources.

children.title: 1.22 Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiaries Medical Assistance Rules
Financial Unit
children.summary: Defines the financial unit for Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiaries (SLMB) medical assistance. The section says the financial unit can be an eligible individual with no spouse, eligible child, eligible couple, or eligible individual with an ineligible spouse. For an eligible individual with no spouse, it includes the Medicare Part A recipient, and if the person is an alien, the sponsor’s income (and spouse’s, if living with sponsor) is counted; SSI recipients are excluded.

children.title: Resource Rules
children.summary: Defines who is included in eligible child, eligible couple, and eligible individual with ineligible spouse cases, noting alien sponsor/spouse income is counted and SSI recipients are excluded. Also specifies household-size rules for resource and income calculations: exclude alien sponsors and their in-home spouses, parents living in the home, ineligible spouses when no deemable income, and SSI recipients. Introduces resource rules as the criteria for classifying household resources as countable or non-countable, and liquid or non-liquid.

children.title: Income Rules
children.summary: This section states the income eligibility rule for Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance (Traditional): only countable income is used, and household income must be greater than 100% and at or below 120% of the Federal Poverty Level. It also notes that income is classified as countable or non-countable, and refers to adult common income rules for calculation steps. This page also begins Qualified Individual Medical Assistance (QI-1) rules, including Medicare Part A, resource, income, and non-financial requirements.

Defines SLMB medical assistance eligibility. To qualify, an individual must meet non-financial requirements, have Medicare Part A, and pass both resource and income tests. The financial unit may be an eligible individual, eligible child, eligible couple, or eligible individual with an ineligible spouse. Alien sponsor/spouse income is counted in specified cases; SSI recipients are excluded. It also sets household-size exclusions for resource and income calculations and introduces resource rules for countable/non-countable, liquid/non-liquid resources.

children.title: 1.23 Qualified Individual Medical Assistance Rules
Financial Unit
children.summary: Defines the financial unit and household size rules for QI-1 Medicare medical assistance. Covers which people are included for eligibility: eligible individual without spouse, eligible child, eligible couple, and eligible individual with ineligible spouse. Notes when sponsor/spouse income is deemed for aliens and that SSI recipients are excluded. Also specifies resource and income household size exceptions, including exclusions for sponsors, parents, ineligible spouses (when no deemable income), and SSI recipients.

children.title: Resource Rules
children.summary: This section summarizes resource and income eligibility rules for Medical Assistance (Traditional), including specific limits ($6,940 for an individual; $10,410 for a couple) and references to related calculation steps. It also introduces Qualified Disabled Working Individuals Medical Assistance (QDWI), explaining that it helps pay Medicare Part A premiums for disabled individuals under 65 who lost premium-free Part A due to work. QDWI requires meeting non-financial requirements, resource and income tests, and having income between 120% and 135% of the federal poverty level.

children.title: Income Rules
children.summary: This section states the income eligibility rules for the program: only countable income is used, and household income must be greater than 120% and no more than 135% of the federal poverty level. It also notes that income types include unearned, earned, and self-employment income, with calculation steps referenced elsewhere. The page also begins the QDWI medical assistance rules, including core eligibility conditions such as age under 65, disability, loss of premium-free Medicare Part A due to work, and meeting resource and income tests.

Defines Qualified Individual (QI-1) Medicare medical assistance eligibility. Covers core requirements: non-financial rules, Medicare Part A, resource test, and income test. States resource limits of $6,940 for an individual and $10,410 for a couple, and income must be above 100% and at or below 120% of FPL for the broader program text shown. Details financial unit and household size rules for eligible individual, child, couple, and individual with ineligible spouse, including sponsor/spouse deeming for aliens and SSI exclusions.

children.title: 1.24 Qualified Disabled Working Individuals Medical Assistance Rules
Financial Unit
children.summary: Defines QDWI financial unit and household size rules. Covers resource limits ($6,940 individual, $10,410 couple) and income rule (countable income must be >120% and ≤135% FPL). Lists allowed financial unit types: eligible individual with no spouse, eligible child, eligible couple, or eligible individual with ineligible spouse. Includes who is counted in each unit, including Medicare Part A recipients, parents for a child, and alien sponsor income in some cases; SSI recipients are excluded.

children.title: Resource Rules
children.summary: Explains Resource Rules for Cúram Income Support Medical Assistance (Traditional): only countable household resources count toward eligibility, with limits of $4,000 for an individual and $6,000 for a couple. Also outlines household size exclusions (e.g., sponsor, parents in home, ineligible spouse with no deemable income, SSI recipients) and notes income must be over 135% and at or below 200% of FPL. Includes a transition to Breast and Cervical Cancer Medical Assistance rules.

children.title: Income Rules
children.summary: This section defines income eligibility rules for Medical Assistance (Traditional). It explains household size exceptions, including excluding an alien sponsor and sponsor’s spouse, parents living in the home, an ineligible spouse when there is no deemable income, and SSI recipients. It also states resource limits ($4,000 individual, $6,000 couple) and notes that only countable resources count. For income, only countable earned, unearned, and self-employment income is used, and household income must be over 135% and at or below 200% of the federal poverty level.

QDWI Medical Assistance rules cover eligibility for disabled workers under age 65 who lost premium-free Medicare Part A due to work and still meet disability, non-financial, resource, and income tests. Financial unit types include single, child, couple, or individual with ineligible spouse; SSI recipients are excluded, and alien sponsor income may count in some cases. Resource limits are $6,940 for an individual and $10,410 for a couple. Income must be above 120% and at or below 135% of FPL.

children.title: 1.25 Breast and Cervical Cancer Medical Assistance Rules
children.summary: Breast and Cervical Cancer Medical Assistance provides full traditional Medicaid for uninsured women under 65 identified through CDC-linked providers who need treatment for breast/cervical cancer, precancerous conditions, or early-stage cancer. Eligibility requires the applicant to be a woman, screened through a Breast and Cervical Cancer Program, meet non-financial requirements, and pass the no creditable insurance test. The section also includes traditional program rules for household size, resources, and income, including resource limits of $4,000 for an individual or $6,000 for a couple and income between 135% and 200% of FPL.

children.title: 1.26 Emergency Medical Assistance Rules
children.summary: Defines Emergency Medical Assistance (EMA) eligibility and coverage. EMA is a short-term, emergency-only Medical Assistance program for non-citizens who otherwise meet MA rules except citizenship/SSN and not qualified alien status. It lists eligible EMA programs, requires EMA qualifying conditions, residency, and an approved emergency medical need, and excludes Refugee Medical Assistance and certain care (organ transplant, routine prenatal/postpartum). Also states application timing, coverage period start/end, and that citizenship rules must not be passed.

children.title: 1.27 Retroactive Medical Assistance Requests
children.summary: Explains how applicants can request retroactive Medical Assistance coverage, either with the initial application or later for existing eligible coverage. Defines the retroactive period as the 3 months before the application month, with eligibility determined separately for each month and requiring a valid medical expense and recorded evidence. Notes that later retroactive requests require prior eligibility and received coverage. Also describes configurable retroactive period start-date behavior and how retroactive eligibility checks are triggered, including exclusions for CHIP and QMB.

Overview of Cúram Income Support for Medical Assistance (Traditional): explains the program purpose, coverage-type rule sets, and eligibility structure. Covers mandatory/optional, emergency, medically needy, long-term care, and Medicare cost-sharing programs; notes post-Jan. 1, 2014 MAGI-based changes and removed cascade items. Describes non-financial rules (citizenship, residency, SSN), assistance-unit formation, financial unit, income/resource rules, cascading eligibility, and retroactive coverage. Includes detailed rules sections for LIFC, ABD, pregnancy, refugee, Medicare savings, BCC, emergency MA, and more.
